main_ingredients: mixed grains (brown rice, quinoa, or similar whole grains)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical grain bowl portion with salmon and four vegetable toppings as served at health-focused food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Grain Bowl (4 Toppings), No Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salmon Grain Bowl (4 Toppings), No Sauce (cr_id F08010052). All per-100g values are taken as reported with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain and whole grain dishes analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salmon grain bowl in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous whole-grain mixed dishes (brown rice GI ~50, quinoa GI ~53, mixed grain bowl with protein and fibre ~40–50 range). The presence of salmon protein (6.4 g/100g), dietary fibre (2.37 g/100g), and absence of sauce further attenuates postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (33.5 g for 350 g portion) / 100 = 15.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 203 mg/100g (710 mg per 350 g serving) approaches the moderate concern threshold for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor total sodium intake from this dish, particularly if the grain base is pre-seasoned; the absence of sauce is beneficial and no additional high-sodium condiments should be added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45), moderate carbohydrate (9.58 g/100g), high dietary fibre (2.37 g/100g), and protein (6.41 g/100g) collectively attenuate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when the grain base is whole grain (brown rice or quinoa); portion size of the grain component should be monitored if blood glucose control is suboptimal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with established cardioprotective effects; low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100g) and dietary fibre (2.37 g/100g) further support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is cardioprotective when salmon is grilled or baked rather than fried; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid adding high-sodium or high-fat sauces and ensure the grain base is whole grain to maximise fibre intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon is a primary dietary source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids with strong anti-inflammatory clinical evidence
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; salmon should be grilled or baked rather than deep-fried, and toppings should avoid nightshade vegetables if individual intolerance is suspected.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon Grain Bowl (4 Toppings), No Sauce is a contemporary health-focused mixed dish popular in Singapore, consisting of a base of mixed whole grains (typically brown rice, quinoa, or a combination) topped with grilled or baked salmon and a choice of four vegetable or salad toppings, served without any sauce or dressing. The dish delivers a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: 94 kcal energy, 6.41 g protein, 3.29 g fat, and 9.58 g carbohydrate, with 2.37 g dietary fibre and 2.78 g sugar. Sodium is 203 mg/100g, which is moderate; the absence of sauce meaningfully reduces the overall sodium load compared to sauced variants. The texture is varied — tender flaked salmon, chewy whole grains, and crisp or lightly cooked vegetable toppings. Clinically, this dish is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low estimated glycaemic index (~45), high fibre, and protein content that collectively moderate postprandial glucose response. The omega-3 fatty acids from salmon (EPA and DHA) provide cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets. It is classified as minimally processed and is a strong candidate for inclusion in health-promotion dietary guidance in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010052) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F08010052 (Salmon Grain Bowl, 4 Toppings, No Sauce) does not report detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), micronutrient panel (vitamins B1–B12, folate, vitamins A/C/D/E), mineral panel (potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), cholesterol, or moisture content as of 2026-04-16; these fields are therefore set to -1 per the Veritas sentinel convention for data not available in the cited approved source. Saturated fat estimated at 0.7 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for analogous salmon and whole grain mixed dish components; this is an approximation and should be verified if precise saturated fat reporting is required. GI is an analogous estimate (~45, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for whole-grain mixed dishes with protein and fibre; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the approved GI database. Halal status assumed based on ingredients (salmon, whole grains, vegetables, no sauce); formal halal certification status of the specific food stall or preparation should be verified independently. Dietary tag 'low_calorie' from input preserved: at 94 kcal/100g this is consistent with a low-calorie classification.
